component_identification.content: Patchwork base components: 1 central time board, 2 quilt boards (one per player), 33 regular patch tiles, 5 special 1x1 leather patches, 1 special 7x7 bonus tile, 1 neutral token, 2 time tokens, and button currency tiles (32x value-1, 12x value-5, 5x value-10, 1x value-20). Each player starts with a quilt board, a time token on start, and 5 buttons. The neutral token is placed near the smallest 1x2 patch and controls market selection among nearby patches.

core_game_loop.content: Patchwork is driven by time-track position, not strict alternation: the player whose time token is behind takes the next turn (so one player may take multiple turns in a row). On a turn, choose exactly one action: advance past opponent to gain buttons, or buy/place one of the next three patches clockwise from the neutral token. Buying a patch costs buttons, consumes time, and increases quilt coverage/income potential. As tokens cross income and special-patch markers, those effects resolve immediately.

detailed_rules.content:
Action A (advance and receive buttons): move your time token to the space directly in front of opponent’s token and gain 1 button per space moved. This is the fallback action and is often strong when available patches are inefficient.

Action B (take and place patch) has five ordered steps: (1) choose one of the three patches immediately clockwise from neutral token; (2) move neutral token to the chosen patch’s former position; (3) pay the printed button cost to supply; (4) place patch on your quilt board without overlap (rotate/flip according to your edition’s allowed placement); (5) move your time token forward by patch time cost.

Time-board events: whenever your token lands on or passes a button-income space, gain buttons equal to your current total printed button income on patches in your quilt. Whenever you are first to pass a special-patch marker, immediately take the associated 1x1 leather patch and place it at once. If tokens land on same space, most recently moved token is placed on top; the behind token always acts next.

7x7 bonus: the first player to completely fill a 7x7 area on their quilt receives the special tile worth +7 points. Only one player can claim it.

Endgame movement cap: if movement would go beyond final time space, stop on final space. In advance action, gain buttons only for actual spaces moved. Game ends when both players reach final space. Practical edge case reminders: if your time token lands exactly on a marker, you still resolve that marker effect; passing over multiple markers in one move resolves each in order. The neutral token always defines the purchase window, so even very cheap patches may become temporarily unavailable until the market rotates. Because the player behind on time acts next, taking a high-time patch can intentionally hand initiative back to the opponent; sometimes that is correct if it secures a uniquely efficient piece. Endgame often swings on empty-square penalties, so late low-cost fillers can outperform high-income patches that arrive too late to pay back.

scoring_and_endgame.content: When both players have reached the final time space, compute final score: buttons remaining (plus +7 if you hold the special 7x7 tile) minus 2 points for every empty square on your quilt board. Higher score wins. Official tie-break: if tied, winner is the player who reached the final space first.

beginner_strategy.content: Tempo beats perfection: a medium patch with low time can be better than a geometrically perfect patch that gives opponent multiple consecutive turns. Invest early in button-income patches so later income triggers fund expensive, high-efficiency pieces. Keep your board shape flexible—avoid isolated pockets that only a rare shape can fill, because each empty square costs 2 points. Passing (advance action) is not failure; it is often the best economic move when the next three patches are overpriced or awkward. Track upcoming special-patch and income markers on the time board and plan purchases so your movement order captures key thresholds before your opponent.

metadata.notes: Rewritten using official rulebook text in /content/rulebook-text as primary source.
